food_name: Penne Pasta with Red Sauce (Vegetarian or Chicken)
region_of_origin: Italy (adapted in Singapore Indian restaurant context)
food_subgroup: Pasta dishes with sauce and optional protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for penne pasta with tomato/red sauce and optional chicken. FOODON_00002473 (pasta food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for penne with red sauce and optional chicken topping.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Pasta dish) used as closest parent; no exact code for penne with red sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of penne pasta with red sauce is approximately 350 g, comprising cooked penne pasta with tomato-based sauce and either vegetables or grilled/sautéed chicken pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA SR Legacy #20121) and Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted (USDA SR Legacy #05064); composite estimate for penne with tomato sauce and optional chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for penne pasta with red tomato sauce and optional chicken as served in a Singapore Indian restaurant. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing: cooked penne pasta (~60% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20121), tomato-based red sauce (~30% by weight, estimated from USDA tomato sauce #11549), and cooked chicken breast (~10% by weight for chicken variant, USDA SR Legacy #05064). Vegetarian variant uses additional vegetables in place of chicken. Values represent a mid-point composite applicable to both variants. USDA FoodData Central is the highest-priority approved secondary global source applicable here, as no HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry covers this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; penne pasta, boiled (GI ~45, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 is based on cooked penne pasta (al dente, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Pasta generally has a low GI (range 32–55) due to its dense starch matrix. The tomato-based red sauce contributes minimal additional glycemic load. GI of 45 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate for this composite dish. GL per serving = (45 × 22.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 35.4 g (serving size 350 g, carbohydrate 22.5 g per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~980 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard restaurant serving of this dish may contribute approximately 980 mg sodium, representing roughly 43% of the 2,300 mg daily limit; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~35 per serving) despite low GI (45) due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although penne pasta has a low glycemic index (~45), the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate (~35 g); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with the chicken variant to increase protein and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g) and tomato-based sauce providing lycopene
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively low in saturated fat and the tomato-based red sauce provides lycopene, a carotenoid associated with cardiovascular protection; the chicken variant adds lean protein without significantly increasing saturated fat, making this a reasonable choice for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when sodium is controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penne Pasta with Red Sauce (Vegetarian or Chicken) is a pasta dish served at Indian-influenced restaurants in Singapore, featuring tube-shaped penne pasta cooked al dente and tossed in a tomato-based red sauce seasoned with garlic, onion, herbs such as basil and oregano, and oil. The dish is available in two variants: a vegetarian version with sautéed vegetables such as capsicum and mushroom, and a chicken version with grilled or sautéed chicken breast pieces. The red sauce is typically a marinara or arrabbiata-style preparation, offering a mildly tangy and herbaceous flavour profile with a smooth, slightly chunky texture. As a pasta dish, penne with red sauce has a low glycemic index (~45) due to the dense starch matrix of the pasta, though the glycemic load per standard restaurant serving (~350 g) is moderate at approximately 35 g. The dish provides a moderate amount of carbohydrate (~22.5 g per 100 g), modest protein (~6.2 g per 100 g, higher in the chicken variant), and low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g). Sodium content is moderate (~280 mg per 100 g) and may be higher depending on restaurant preparation. The tomato sauce contributes lycopene, vitamin C, and potassium. Clinically, this dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and prefer the chicken variant for additional protein to moderate postprandial glucose response. Those managing hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation. The dish is halal-friendly and free of dairy in its standard form.
common_user_queries: Is penne pasta with red sauce healthy?
What is the calorie count of penne pasta with tomato sauce?
Is penne pasta good for diabetics?
Penne pasta red sauce nutrition Singapore
Penne pasta chicken vs vegetarian calories

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact entry for penne pasta with red sauce as served in a Singapore Indian restaurant context. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta, tomato sauce, and cooked chicken breast. GI is based on analogous cooked penne pasta data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation is methodologically sound and uses approved sources, but exact restaurant-specific values may vary.
notes: 1. This dish is listed under 'Indian' cuisine on field survey data (Dugout - Circular Road), but penne pasta with red sauce is an Italian-origin dish adapted into the menu of an Indian restaurant in Singapore. The cuisine field reflects this dual context. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy approved sources; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation, sauce recipe, oil quantity, and protein variant chosen. 3. The chicken variant will have higher protein_g (estimated ~9–11 g per 100 g) and slightly higher energy_kcal; values presented represent a mid-point composite. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for pasta and tomato sauce components; chicken breast also contributes negligible trans fat). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite sources used for this estimation. 6. Sodium estimate (~280 mg per 100 g) is based on a lightly salted tomato sauce preparation; restaurant versions may be significantly higher. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate for cooked penne pasta from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be marginally lower due to protein and fat contributions from sauce and chicken. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 100 = (45 × 22.5 × 350) / 100 = 35,437.5 / 100 ≈ 35.4 g. 9. is_vegetarian is set to false at the record level because the dish has a chicken variant; the vegetarian variant would qualify as vegetarian. 10. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish requirements (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore.
